Three years later and I thought I might now view this video. I guess we know about using aluminium or calcium carbide as the energy source to react with water to produce hydrogen and acetylene as a fuel. A fella was reported to have driven across USA by means of calcium carbide produced acetylene maybe fifty years ago. 
  In Australia we had a Yul Brown who was a Hungarian immigrant who developed basically  a technique to store hydrogen and oxygen together under pressure which is a slight safety concern but anyway it was more that he was claiming incredible mileage figures for vehicles fuelled by his gas set-up . It was around 1980 and the Premier of Queensland Bjelke Petersen wanted to back his work. Yul Brown was claiming that fusion was occuring in the combustion chamber of the engine verifed by mentioning that helium levels in the exhaust were higher than atmospheric concentration of helium.
    And so in the last few years an Australian author historian name of Roland Perry has written a novel which might be classed as SciFi detective story about called The Shaman. An inventor of Aboriginal/Hungarian descent Invents a cold fusion device for powering motor vehicles.
  I think Rob would have been interested in this story several years ago.
  Valé Robert.

author: @mtttony
text:
Thanks for taking the time to look through my work. 
I got started on this in 2007 and ten years later the penny dropped.
My whole life I have had a great passion for how things work. And would rather read an instruction manual than a book. So strap in, this is quite a revelation, 
Stanley Meyer and his twin brother Stephen were industrial problem solvers. Stephen has an impeccable resume and has been involved in many industrial developments with inventions and patents like the control of dielectric coatings on magnet wire, grading of fresh orange juice, if you drink orange juice it has been through his patented grading process. He was called out to put out a gas well fire in Libya that was over a mile high, using jet engines fired by the gas from the well below and directing the non combustible exhaust gases into the flame to extinguish them. So when Red Adair is not around they would call Stephen. 
Back to problem solving. As young brothers Stan and Stephen would sit after dinner time, and propose a problem that had to be solved. To do so they would split the pros and cons between them. One would spend ten minutes proposing positive steps to the problem and the other would spend ten minutes proposing the problems in doing that and then take turns about doing this into the early hours of the morning till they could come up with a viable answer.
Ok on to the matter at hand Stan and his New Atomic Energy source and his water powered car.
The first thing I would like to point out is that electrolysis has very little to do with this new technology. And it can be done without any electrolysis. There has been a bit of a Stanley Meyer cult build up over the years with many people trying to make electrolysis more efficient with the hope of some magic frequency or combinations to replicate Stan's work. This is clearly a byproduct of when looking at Stan's system people grab onto the only thing that they are familiar with. Disregarding the rest of the parts of the system with it all being an unknown.
Ok here’s the rundown of the different applications of this new discovery.  A new clean fuel that can be retrofitted to a combustion engine. A way to make superheated steam with very little influx of amps. A hydrogen laser many times the power of the status quo. An electrical particle generator with no moving parts that amplifies the input amps. A way to bypass normal chemical chains and make hundreds of new compounds. A way to reduce radioactive waste down to a safe atomic structure and make electricity through his new reduction method. 
What I am about to explain you need to have a basic understanding in a number of fields but the good news is really only up to A Level for the most part. Remember Stan says anyone can build this in their garage or shed. 
If you look at pictures of Stan's car you will see he is using it as a testing platform for all of the patented arrangements. All bar one were for legal reasons, as he was only going to be taking his water fuel injection system to market. On the car you will see a gas management system on the dashboard. You will see a stainless steel box to the right of the engine with 11 VIC units inside voltage intensifier circuits. You will see a waterfuel holding tank with a water level sensor and a steam resonator to never let the water cool below freezing. Top most center of the engine you will see what he called his Gas Processor. Pictures and relevant videos for explanation sake are available online.
I'm going to start with the oxygen atom/molecule. Oxygen is unique as it is used to start chemical reactions given it will gain electrons from other atoms, and so when starting a reaction in the case of steel it will gain an electron from the steel starting the process we call rust, oxidation.
A hydrogen atom has one electron and is one of the most basic of atom structures. Keeping it all as simple as possible.
At this point I urge you to spend half an hour looking into gas ionisation techniques at A level learning to understand the circuit and the effects of Townsend Avalanche on gases.
Also basic electrical tank circuit. Electrostatic fields like the ones used in a cyclotron or used in a CRT TV to control the positioning of the electrons from the electron gun to hit the backside of the screen and hit the fluorescent material coating to give off light emissions. Is it not true that to increase the light density of the fluorescent material,brightness, you just increase the B+ voltage of the  electrostatic field to increase the speed at which the electron hits the fluorescent substrate. Also a basic understanding of the action in a fluorescent tube light. All of this is very relevant to the process. Stan uses circuit resonance for the only reason that giving the components used, resonance is the most efficient way to utilize the components.
At this point I would like to give some direction on how the twins approached the problem.
As we know Faraday electrolysis is well known to be very inefficient. Using around three times the electrical energy per unit of energy from the gases created from water. Now here is the part that is never disused. You are told that when you burn the hydrogen and oxygen from water you get energy out and water.. Stan did a study with NASA and came to the conclusion that the water produced when combusting hydrogen and oxygen must be looked at as a byproduct of the reaction. If looked into more closely you will find that only one millionth of the gases are combusted and the rest of your hard earned gases turn straight to water. Giving this process a very low efficiency level. So Stan and Stephen asked themselves how we could improve on this extreme inefficiency..  So they turned to. What if we were to increase the energy level of the air going into the process? Would this make any significant gains with efficiency? 
Ionising the air.
Ok, his Gas Processor / air ioniser. A basic understanding of how air can be ionised with electrostatic fields. Most commonly known for the production of ozone. 
An air ioniser does not make ozone inside the device. Ozone is made as the gases leave the ioniser and only then they join up.. Oxygen molecules get drawn into the ionizer and between the electrostatic field electrodes instantly get split into oxygen atoms, electrons get ejected and electron sharing starts to happen.
On exiting the ioniser the oxygen atoms quickly bond into molecules again and can carry a third oxygen atom. Forming ozone with a very short life. When put into contact with anything of an opposite charge, they drop the third oxygen atom and it does work oxidising like a gaseous bleach..

author: @mtttony
text:
Stan redesigned the air ioniser. He knew if he could make more reactive oxygen it may increase the efficiency of the hydrogen and oxygen energy output during combustion. 
What he did, was to use the electrostatic fields of air ioniser to break down the oxygen molecules from air. But also prevent the formation of oxygen molecules and ozone post ioniser..
 Well how exactly did he do this? 
To do this he understood that if you have two electrostatic electrodes of opposite polarity with a dielectric in between being air. Then do you not have a capacitor?  When you break the molecules apart into monatomic atoms ions and free floating electrons there is an amount of charge on that capacitor.
If the pulsing circuit is interrupted and the capacitor is discharged through a filament that produces thermal energy as light. You can burn off free floating electrons from the oxygen atoms. And in so make an Oxygen Cation production unit. Not just Oxygen Cations but Cations with up to four missing electrons. This is a very reactive oxygen atom. In order to enhance this process Stan added pulsed light emissions to the gases in the processor 180 degrees out of phase to the electrostatic pulsing. This temporarily added energy to the oxygen atoms and made them more likely to eject electrons and less likely to gain electrons in their unstable state. The light added was very simple 660 Nm LEDs targeting the oxygen atoms.. So now he has an Oxygen Cation production unit. Keep in mind Oxygen Cations are not neutral thay are positively charged particles and in so are accelerated towards a negative electrostatic field and repelled by a positive electrostatic field. So do keep this in mind! His VIC circuits are High voltage particle accelerators.
So here comes the new discovery the twins made!
When mixing the Oxygen Cations into the combustion of hydrogen and oxygen gas from electrolysis or bottled hydrogen and oxygen. Hence no need for the Faraday electrolysis. They had anomalous energy output. But not just from increasing the efficiency of the gas combustion. They stumbled upon an energy output over and above gas combustion.. They called this Hydrogen Fracturing! 
Now as we know Oxygen is eight times the size of a hydrogen atom. And in so has eight times the attraction force over an electron compared to the hydrogen atom. So if there is a free floating electron the oxygen cation will gain it, and the hydrogen proton will have to wait to find another electron. But what if the hydrogen proton can't find an electron to stabilize? 
Well as we know the combustion moment is extremely quick and inherently wants to shut down and stop releasing energy and go on to form the water molecule. Stans gas mixing of the Cations into the hydrogen and oxygen gases from water or bottles postponed the formation of the water molecule as the Cations remove / gain all of the available electrons during combustion. This forces the hydrogen protons without an electron to implode. The implosion of the hydrogen proton makes a shockwave that can be directed on to the top of a piston. E=MC2 Mass to energy. This is a new atomic energy release, over and above gas combustion. And as there is no neutron interaction then it is a clean process. One hydrogen atom has very little amount of energy but if you have many. They all add up..
This process is totally controllable by way of mixing the amount of gas Cations hence the GMS, Gas Management System in the dash of the car. So are you still with me lol!! It’s a lot to take in given I am dropping you in at the deep end..
Ok the next invention using the cations is quite amazing. I impart this information to you, and give you the duty to pass it on. Anyway here we go..
The next discovery the twins made. Is that if you are to dissolve the Oxygen Cations into water. This is where I was positioned to understand this as I worked on a fish farm for twenty years and broke records for transporting live fish across land with minimum mortalities. Constantly adjusting the dissolved oxygen from tanks into the water to keep the fish alive…This is how Stan made and coined the name. Waterfuel, oxygen cations in the water.

author: @mtttony
text:
As we know water can hold up to 17% dissolved gases, like in a can of coke.
Stan dissolved the gas oxygen cations into water. He discovered that these oxygen cations would re-emerge during his improved electrolysis method using high voltage pulses to release hydrogen and oxygen from water called the voltage or electrical polarisation process. The cations can not gain electrons from the water molecules as they don’t give up their electrons easily. And so are held in state. When the oxygen cations re-emerged into the gas production gases. What do you have? A cation is an ionised gas. Ionised particles in the gas production. By way of high voltage electrostatic fields ions can be accelerated through the gas production bubbles. If vibrated back and forth. They start a Townsend Avalanche and ionise the gas production bubbles.. So what exactly happens when you have ionised gas bubbles surrounded by water. Well the ionised gases shoot high speed particles emission, electrons and gas ions into the walls of the water. Water molecules are held together by way of the covalent electrons. If you remove the covalent electron. You instantly break apart the molecule, into hydrogen and oxygen. Faraday electrolysis pulls the water molecule apart with the use of amps. But if you can ionise the water molecule in a physical manner and knock off an electron it will split and they can not reform.Having ionised gas bubbles that are firing high speed particles into the water that is surrounding them. Knocks off electrons from the water molecules. And in a new physical manner splits the water molecules. This is bringing more charged particles into the frame to be accelerated. This action happens in an exponential curve. The same as Townsend Avalanche is exponential. Stan and Stephens gas production was also exponential. Stan said he could make so much gas from his cell he couldn't fill it with water quick enough. That’s a lot of gas…Like I said back at the beginning. Stan was only going to take his waterfuel injector, to market. Reason being there is minimal gas production. All gas production and GTNT/Hydrogen Fracturing is inside the injector cavity. This is inside the engine where it replaced the spark plug. So it was safe and fool proof. If you can make an energy release over and above gas combustion then the less gas burnt the better.. To give you an idea of the energy conversion during hydrogen fracturing. One US gallon of water equals 6 million barrels of oil or thereabouts. Einstein will back this up. Stan could use hundreds of times less gas combustion to produce the same amount of energy to move a piston.His super heated steam production for use in many applications including desalination and well anything you can use superheated steam for. Superheated steam can be made by using either one electrostatic polarity or two alternate electrostatic polarity poles on the waterfuel.
With the use of only one electrostatic field at one time. Stan could pull the gas cations through the water. This would cause them to bump into the water molecules and flex the bonds. Much in the same way a microwave flexes the bonds. Causing the bonds to produce heat. Stan is only using one electrode at any given moment to move the charged particles through the water.  How much energy are you going to use to create an electrostatic field? Especially when the circuit has no return path. You will see his steam resonator on the car and you will see his home heating boiler in pictures online.
As you can imagine I know this is a hell of a lot to take in in one sitting. And is the kind of subject that can be absorbed in a conversation pulling up the relevant documents and videos and pictures over the course of some time.I have tried to condense twenty years of Stan's work into a few paragraphs. Stan had a saying: KISS , keep it simple stupid. This might seem a bit out of place with what I am explaining to you. But in the context of a new atomic energy that can be done at home in a garage or shed. It is keeping it as simple as possible..I hope this offers you a key insight into the understanding you need to study Stan's mountain of work that he left behind for us all. He even made sure to legalise it. So it could not be prohibited from public use in the future for us all. All the best, Ps once it all sinks in, and it might take a while. Please pass it on as there are many people out there that are great at building. But only if they know just exactly what they need to build.. Cheers Tony..

It’s more efficient to run an electric motor to the wheels than a cavitation device. Long time fan, your videos are great!

No, you should try first running a moped liquid cooled, without cooling, with a carburetor that vaporizes the gas through the coolant channels and back into the intake. 
Its simple. 
Use less fuel. Make less heat. 
Use water injection, or even a water dedicated carburetor in series. SO one does gas, one does water in fine mist, then it all gets vaporized and turned into steam and then will combust more completely and cool. With steam working more towards the end of the travel of the piston on the down cycle. You could have more power. I would try it specifically on a moped or motorcylce because they are cheap rugged, easy to work on and easy to check everything, power, fuel consumption, etc. 
Try first with just vapor of gas, then add water if it needs it. If it atomized more, it is more efficient it is just that simple. Vapor is more efficient than direct injection.

author: @matildastanford7019
text:
Methane is not necessarily a fossil fuel.
Methane can be easily produced with an anaerobic bacteria,  quite renewable and released by a few natural processes.  
However it is a greenhouse gas,  so extracting methane for hydrocarbon petrochemistry or hydrogen extraction is actually quite beneficial.
Additionally methane can be reduced to CO + H2,  syngas,  which can be used to synthesize cleaner hydrocarbon fuels and monomer chemicals.

Sorry for the rant but l think it's crucial to make the distinctions between naturally occuring pollution,  fossil fuels and sustainable fuel sources.

author: @chaorrottai
text:
All I know is that the second law of thermodynamics is invalidated by the carnot equation for the efficiency of heat pumps.

Since heat pump are real devices and obey the behaviour stipulated in the carnot equation, heat pumps are capable of moving heat energy from one heat sink to the other heat sink with a COP that is often greater than one, which is a deceptive way of saying it's over 100% efficient.

The carnot equation stipulates the maximum Q factor for a heat driven engine is "Q = (Tmax -Tmin) / Tmax" where temperature is in kelvin, which is why gas motors and heat motors can never exceed that Q factor let alone reach 100% efficiency, most of the time, when a motor is rated for it's efficiency it's rated against the carnot efficiency, not 100% efficiency as it's understood that this is just litterally impossible to attain. This plays nice with the second law of thermodynamics because entropy is increasing in this scenario no matter what temperature differential is used. However, heat engines like carnot engines are reversible.

When they are reversed or you are using the refrigerations cycle then the Q factor is the inverse of the heat engine. The efficiency of relocating heat energy is determined by the formula "Q = Tmax / (Tmax - Tmin)". This means that the denomitator is always going to be smaller than numerator meaning the efficiency of heat transfer from one sink to the other sink will always exceed 100%. This is a decrease in entropy since more energy is being ordered than is being disordered.

Entropy is all about differential energy when there is no differential energy like in a dead battery or in a isotropically heated room, the entropy is at it's maximum. But when the differential energy is high, like with a fully charged battery or two rooms of very different temperature, then the entropy is low. Well the heat pump does, that. It lowers entropy faster than the rate of increase of entropy caused by running the heat pump. If I have a 12 watt-hour battery and I run it to an air conditioner with a COP of 2.2 then when the battery is dead, I will have created 26.4 watt-hours worth of differential energy between the sinks of the heat pump.


The second law of thermodynamics states that the entropy of a closed system can NEVER decrease, heat pumps exists and have proven opperation, therefore the second law of thermodynamics is incorrect.

It uses a very old system of physical mechanical linkage to make and break contact with the electrolysis  contacts.  It looks like it uses a motor to move a gear that will move the contact to make or break contacts with what the throttle position is so as to keep up the ' bubble ' rate for the intake of the carb and not stall out.  The remainder of the carburetor ( or carb ) appears similar to what is currently used for LNG cars ( propane in most places ) with safety valves and a form of spark arrest baffle to prevent explosive blowback from the cylinders.
The one image for the water powered dune buggy looks like a modified gas injector where there is also an induction coil making it both the injector and that cylinders' coil adding to your theory that he was using the cavitation effect to fire the pistons.

author: @Drjtherrien
text: Many moons ago in my foolish teenage years I was convinced that the future was cars running on electrolyzed water. Now Robert points out many reasons this doesn't make sense, but there is a major one that really kills the idea of that kind of "water car": Thermodynamics. If you electrolyze water you are really converting the energy stored in a battery into chemical energy (yes... if you think that through enough it's even more ridiculous; electrical->chemical->electrical->chemical). That chemical energy is then used in an inefficient heat engine to form mechanical energy. Instead skip those steps and go from the battery to an electric motor which has higher efficiency to begin with.

text:
No doubt there are lots of viable ways to convert energy from one source into movement using water as an intermediate / electrolyte / fuel.

But what's the point in doing that when the energy source you're using to fanny about with water can just be used directly?

A HHO / electrolysis /cavitation car is just an electric car with a less efficient powertrain than using the energy source directly to drive an electric motor.

Electric motors are very efficient already, and the other powertrains have the disadvantage that getting rid of the electric motor means you can't use it for regenerative braking, don't take advantage of the much easier maintenance (compared to combustion), have to have a gearbox (to stay within the power curve of a combustion engine) etc.

The issue of electrolisis vs. the laws of thermodynamics (conservation of energy/matter) is based on the assumption that electrolisis consumes more energy than is released when the hydrogen produced is recombined with oxygen in an exothermic reaction, and that conventional electrolisis is the only way to fracture water molecules into their elemental componenets. Thermodynamics, a physical law that is inviolable, would seem to support that assumption. But since all matter is fundamentally energy in a highly organized configuration, are we simplifying things too much? Could it be that we do not really understand where the energy potential in water, or in any substance, actually lies? Science is a voyage of ongoing discovery, and we can’t assume that what we now understand (or think we understand) is the absolute truth. Prior to the discovery of nuclear energy and the enormous energy potential in atoms, people had no idea of the true nature of the substances that we take for granted. Are there other energy sources that we don’t even know about?
Stan Myers demonstrated that a very small input of electrical energy in a particular configuration could produce large quantities of combustible gas, in an apparent contradiction of the 1st Law of Thermodynamics. So, apparently, we’ve actually got it wrong. Thermodynamics hasn’t changed, its where the energy is coming from that is in question.

replies.author: @lloydevans2900
replies.text:
@DOCTOR_SONGWhat makes the difference for whether alkaline batteries can be safely recharged or not has more to do with the physical construction of the cell than the electrochemistry going on inside: Both zinc-carbon and alkaline cells use metallic zinc as the negative electrode, and that zinc metal is consumed as the cell is discharged. Some cheaper cells use only that zinc metal as the outer casing of the cell, and these are the cells that you probably shouldn't try to recharge using any of the chargers designed to recharge alkaline or zinc-carbon cells.
Why? Because as the cell is discharged, that zinc casing gets progressively thinner as the zinc is consumed from the inside, which is why those cells can often leak when they are discharged all the way to empty - the zinc consumption is not always evenly spread out, so heavy discharge can eat all the way through the zinc casing, making extremely weak spots or even holes in the casing. The charging process (which should re-form metallic zinc, again on the inside of the casing) is not going to close up the holes, because there has to be some zinc present for the electrolytic reaction to deposit more zinc metal onto.

Also, the re-forming of zinc metal during charging is not going to be completely evenly spread out across the entire inner surface either. If the first use of the cell has made weak spots, the recharging process will add zinc to those weak spots, but even when charging is complete, those weak spots will not be restored to their full original thickness, so the next discharge can then create holes where those weak spots are, even if the first discharge didn't make any holes. So it's not that the recharging chemistry isn't going to work for every cell, it's just that repeated charge/discharge cycles on cheaper cells which only have zinc casings is highly likely to make holes in the casing after a few cycles, causing the cells to leak, which isn't particularly safe.

However, higher quality zinc-carbon or alkaline cells use a steel casing. The chemistry going on inside is still the same - there is still a layer of zinc metal which is consumed when the cell is discharged and then re-formed when the cell is recharged. The difference is that even if a cell is heavily discharged and the zinc metal is completely consumed in some places, this will not make holes in the cell - it still has that outer steel casing so is far less likely to leak.
Because of this, these higher quality cells can safely be recharged many times. It is easy enough to tell the difference between the cheaper and higher quality cells: If they stick to a magnet, then they have the steel outer casing. This test can even be done without opening the packaging new cells are sold in, so you can just take a magnet with you and test any new cells before you buy them.

replies.author: @markiobook8639
replies.text: You miss the point atmospheric air is the working fuel of internal combustion engine- superheated air+exhaust  expands, propelling the directionally limited piston downward to a crank which converts linear motion to rotational. On the power stroke shortly after ignition the exhaust valve opens hot exhaust escapes, inlet valve then opens allowing the ambient atmospheric air to equilibriate pressure and traditionally air is mixed with fuel on intake, the intake and exhaust valve are closed, then compression stroke commences by either momentum (via crank weight slightly off bottom dead centre) for single cylinder or powered by other cylinders is reproduced.

replies.author: @edbennett8257
replies.text: @markiobook8639 Congratulations. You have just demonstrated a complete misunderstanding of the actual operating principles of an ICE. Air is not the "working fuel", rather it is the working MEDIUM. It is also the carrier of the oxygen that, when exposed to atomized combustible hydrocarbons (AKA fuel) and ignited participates in a rapid chemical reaction (combustion) generating heat and and a rapid increase in volume of the working medium which drive the piston down in the cylinder.  Also, the exhaust valve does not open "shortly after ignition allowing the ambient atmospheric air to equilibriate pressure". The exhaust valve opens very close to the bottom of the stroke, as late as possible after ignition to allow the expanding gasses in the cylinder to perform as much work as possible for maximum efficiency while still purging all spent gasses from the cylinder.

replies.author: @markiobook8639
replies.text:
@edbennett8257 I never said it was the working fuel I said it was the working fluid- oh no I think I was autocorrected. Criticism taken. But in essence we are in agreement- we differ in the point at which valves open- which is a timing/dwell issue and not necessarily top or bottom centre is the basis of good timing. For early engines it was a significant problem of continuing crank rotation of the piston at top and bottom dead centre in early engines. Sometimes it happens with newly rebuilt engines- they will ignite and exhaust then that's it. 
additionally on the issue of timing TDC and BDC- antique cars would not have advance-retard adjustors nor rich-lean adjustors- which have since been replaced by computerisation if mechanical timing is adequate. The reality is because of rapidity there is an overlap of opening/closing valves- it is only the power stroke that both are closed definitively otherwise either or they are open by degree.

replies.author: @edbennett8257
replies.text: @markiobook8639 You continue to display your lack of understanding. Read up some more on the subject. FWIW, there is only one place in the four stroke ICE cycle that both valves are open at the same time. This overlap occurs at the top of the exhaust stroke/beginning of the intake stroke. It is there to allow the inertia of the exhaust gasses flowing from the  cylinder to actually pull intake charge into the cylinder, and only occurs for a relatively short time in order to increase the efficiency of the engine. Also, both valves remain closed for the entirety (less a few degrees at the beginning and end) of the compression and the power strokes. If either valve is not fully sealed during the compression stroke, a loss of compression and poor efficiency is the result. If the exhaust valve opens much before the end of the compression stroke, the pressure pushing the piston down is lost immediately, again resulting in poor efficiency. While variable valve timing is now a common feature, it isn't nearly as dramatic as you seem to believe and merely adjusts the open/close of the valves a few degrees for best performance at various engine speed/load conditions. The same is also true of computerized fuel injection and ignition timing. Up until computerized controls became commonplace, all of these parameters were fixed by mechanical components, and compromises had to be made to average the performance across the operating range for acceptable performance. Newer operating systems can optimize the engine performance much better for a wider variety of operating conditions, but the underlying physics and operating principles remain the same. And, in response to your first response, I did not "miss the point". He was positing water as fuel, but then said that electrical energy would have to be added to it to produce the cavitation. By definition, "fuel" is the source of the energy input into the system, not the working medium that the fuel acts upon.

text:
There are many studies from the greatest automotive producers concerning water addition especially in diesel engines. They all found much cleaner exhaust emissions, primarily less soot. In older diesel engines with prechambers higher efficiency resp. less fuel consumption as well, but poorly in modern diesel engines with direct injection.
Unfortunately water additive causes a number of problems: preformed fuel-water emulsions tend to separation and fouling which means clogging of tubing and filters. Furthermore corrosion in the engine is an issue, because stainless steel is poorly usable for engines due to a lack of strenght and high thermal expansion.
"Water engines" are very interesting at first glance - but lead to many problems in practice.

author: @greenaum
text:
Cavitation explosions use either electricity to create intense heat in a small space, or else shrimp muscle power. The water isn't a "fuel", it's a medium. Nobody's been working on perpetual motion water-powered cars because any scientist who'd get into college in the first place already knows that it's impossible. Thermodynamics, my boy. Which isn't anomalous, even if you don't have a great understanding of it personally. 

If Archimedes really did move the world with a big lever, people would be looking for all sorts of miracles, but it'd really just be an old Greek with a big lever. Cavitation isn't an exception to physics, and you'd need one for water to be a fuel. Well, you can always react it with something very reactive. But getting hold of, and dealing with, that substance would be a big deal. And even so water wouldn't be the "fuel" any more than the very-reactive substance would be. Any more than air is a "fuel" for a normal car.

author: @greenaum
text:
As far as "Brown's Gas", HHO, free hydrogen can only exist as ions, an unlikely state to persist in for long, so it's like to just be simple H2 and O2, 2xH2 and 1xO2 for every 2xH2O, of course. Proposed hydrogen cars don't burn it though, they use it in fuel cells to generate electricity directly using a membrane in a way that's sort of halfway between electrolysis backwards and osmosis. As you mention, free hydrogen isn't found anywhere on Earth so it's nearly all made from methane, in a process that releases CO2, and methane itself is a greenhouse gas, 25x as bad as CO2, so any large scale distribution networks are gonna have little leaks that are going to add up. 

Basically the whole thing was a smokescreen for the oil industry while they did nothing and twiddled their thumbs for a few years, til they could think up their next distraction. Worked too well though because it brought credibility and attention to electric cars.

You should also be really wary on this subject cos there's SO many people who are certain perpetual motion is possible using technology that's been around for 200 years, but that those people themselves don't understand properly, having only learned of electrolysis through enthusiastic Youtube videos from others who either don't have a proper clue, or are after selling something. Meanwhile anyone who paid attention during high school science is a shill for oil companies. Which I bloody wish, I bet it pays well. 

Yes you could electrolyse hydrogen to squirt into your engine, but that electricity comes from the alternator, which is turned by the engine, which requires fuel. And the more electricity you draw (saw, to split some water molecules), the more load it places on the engine, and therefore the more fuel the engine uses to keep turning. So you're "extracting" energy from your own petrol. But putting it through a few needless and inefficient processes first. So INCREASING fuel use.

Honestly, you have to talk to these people like they're toddlers, though it won't help. As far as they understand any of it, they won't accept it.

replies.author: @grantfrith9589
replies.text:
That is an idea that I've been wrestling with for a number of years. Humidity in the air is not quite right. It actually has the opposite of the intended effect on modern cars.

Having said that there are a couple of avenues I think might be a way to get benifits from adding water to the combustion cycle. 

The first is conceivable that in older vehicles before electronic ignition is that the suppressed burn and cooling effect means you can advance the timing and gain a consequently  cleaner burn without burning holes in your pistons. I think with the early attempts of onboard electrolysis that it was actually the water vapor from inefficient hydrogen cells rather than the tiny amounts of hydrogen generated that gave some tinkerers the results they reported.

The second avenue intrigues my but it's all hypothesis, and that is rather than saturated air you use cooler air that the water is like clouds in that it's  condensed into a mist. The idea is that the micro droplets when converting to steam make the combustion cycle more thermal efficient.

It would take a bit of tinkering to get right if it's possible to make it work without using turbo boost or water injection systems but it's definitely something that I want to explore at some point.

What vehicle did you do your experiments on?

replies.author: @rallen7660
replies.text:
Back during WWII there was an extreme fuel shortage in the US, Europe, and Australia. With the extreme shortage of fuel, and the rationing that it caused, it put a big damper on young men and women going out on late night rides to "watch the submarine races". Never get in the way of hormones, you'll lose every time. This was the motivation for the development of "Wood Gas". Anyone that's watched logs burn on a fire has seen it: when a green-ish piece of wood gets dropped on a bed of coals, then you see steamy gasses escape from the log before they burst into flames. As my uncle told me as a child, "Smoke is unburned fuel". A little bit of study shows that wood seems to burn at two different temperature ranges, around 4-600 degrees Fahrenheit, and then the carbon monoxide generated from the coals burns at 1292 degrees Fahrenheit. The common way of generating this gas seemed to be a "converter" that would be batch loaded with woodchips, then burned in a slow, low oxygen environment, as if you were making charcoal. Now what a lot of people don't remember is that arclights were a big deal at the same time. Basically, they were using carbon arc gouge rods connected to a welder and using that as a light source (SUPER BRIGHT). If you put the resulting arc underwater, you generate a ton of carbon monoxide and Hydrogen gas without tearing into the woodpile. The "Water Engine" does go through water, but it goes through carbon electrodes a lot faster, and that's the fuel for the engine. Wood Gas doesn't produce the same amount of power as gasoline, maybe about 75% as much, but it doesn't require an insane amount of infrastructure  either. So if you have access to cheap wood mulch, it might be a viable fuel source for home power generation.

As a side note: Carbon Monoxide combustion is the secret to the super high efficiency of the Russian fireplace, and the Rocket Stoves that everyone is playing with. Remember: Physics is your friend!

I have always been fascinated with Stan Meyers tech for some reason.
I have a playlist of videos regarding him

https://m.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLNY9F364UuoMgtJrlvOFlqXXt_YJFcQOX

The best examination by him is his Auckland house lecture https://youtu.be/yGqCaVFWIWQ

Stan had several systems. No were electrolyzers. They were more "water bond crackers". The big difference was the water never used any electrolyte. No salt  added. In fact the water needed to be an insulator because basically made a radio tank circuit with the water as the capacitors insulator and. Diode in the circuit so each pulse increased electric static pressure on the water molecule bonds. The difference between hitting a nail with a hammer versus pushing it with your hand.
His fraud case concerns fundraising and was a letter of the law case. It never showed his machine didn't work.

His first patent was for the tank cracker. His later ones were miniaturized enough to fit the spark plug holes of an engine.

His house lecture video is worth watching and remember if you ever see anyone add any electrolyte to the water it's the opposite of a Meyer system.

author: @C-M-E
text:
I pursued the cavitation route with a supplementary reaction for use in modified jet engines as stationary gas generators (matter of fact I have the prototype and its successor collecting dust behind me in my office) and as you might imagine, there were and still are some problems to overcome, mostly material engineering at this stage. It's actually what led me to my side project of making graphene additives for composite parts and a few other things, but chiefly the reaction causes brutal metal embrittlement. In my process, it's doubly as bad as a piston engine, as you have the cavitation process and the further combustion phase down the line, exceptionally high temperatures that I've yet to find any aerospace alloy that can cope (hence the departure into ceramics and composites), and the lack of transferable manufacturing processes outside of prototyping capacity, even with all the advances in additive manufacturing. 
Eventually I'll get all my ducks lined up, but every time I make some serious headway, I stumble into a new material application that grabs my interest. There's been several years where I should have pursued one linear direction, though I must admit that getting 'Stan Meyered' is a high concern. Funnily enough, 30 or 40 years ago, the idea of all these magical inventions like the automotive grade 100mpg carburetor being bought and shelved by the oil companies seemed like a distant myth. Now its far more practical to ruin someone's reputation than to ever anticipate Big Oil coming like Daddy Warbucks to your door to buy your miracle product.

The huge sums of money he needed to get his ideas implemented was the biggest obstacle to him and made it difficult to find investors. He was just lucky to be rich enough able to back himself.

replies.author: @C-M-E
replies.text:
@William_Hada Believe me, I wish it were enough these days to just have a good idea, but without some financial standing or a platform that you've garnered some kind of following, you will have a difficult time at best getting your idea noticed by the right people. They'll be plenty of those beating down your door saying you're a fool and it'll never work, who will also go to the ends of the earth to attempt to disprove you as your idea threatens their way of thinking, which will come not only from industry but also the scientific community and public at large. 
End note, I'm not saying it can't be done, but look at the risks from all sides before letting your butterfly into the world teeming with spiders and webs.

author: @xjet
text:
The reason we don't have water-powered cars is simple:   The laws of thermodynamics are immutable.

Remember that water is the ash you get when you burn hydrogen.  The bond between the hydrogen and oxygen atoms makes it very difficult to disassociate water into its component elements.  In effect, the amount of energy released by burning hydrogen (2(H2)+O2 -> 2(H20))  is the same amount required to then dissociate 2(H20) into 2(H2)+O2.    Water is effectively spent hydrogen fuel and just as you can't burn the ash in your fireplace -- you can't get any more energy out of water.

author: @brettjasonheadinventor
text:
Nice video. As an inventor in this field, I can confirm that water is not a fuel in it's normal states. To produce energy from water it needs to be decomposed or as you mentioned propelled.  The energy consumed to perform electrolysis compared to the energy produced is predicted (Faraday Laws ).  Applying those laws proves these devices don't work. Devices like GEET may work as its not connected to a external power source, however, they never explained it simply. 

My invention is a hybrid cross between a EGR and a  water decomposing  vapour system. It  simply generates static electricity from  redirected exhaust gas to produce intermediate electrostatic discharges to predominately  provide a power source to boost  electrolysis. A small ( under 6 watts  )  external power source  is used to run the electronics and to electrically charge the electrodes. Cooling, treating exhaust gas and flowing it through silicone tubing increases the generation of static electricity. The process also  produces new water for filtering the gas and produces  acid  electrolyte solution. Only exhaust gas is used as the working medium. 

Overall this process produces combustible and combustion supporting substances above the Faraday predicted outcome. 

The moist gaseous mixture is then fumigated into the engine as demanded by the engine. 
It's a real device being tested and used on some vehicles here in Australia and UK and France by real engineers, including an electromechanical engineer who works for Tesla.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Water for fuel in reguard to HHO, is a misnomer as you point out. it's hydrogen gass (fuel) that is being oxidized. Even then, most often it's generated in relatively small quantities compared to the primary fuel.

And I'd argue, where it's used in small quantities, it's primary purpose is less about adding more fuel, and more about increasing the speed of the flame front at ignition. I'd say, the only idea worth merit is a little hydrogen helps more fully burn the primary fuel early in the power stroke were it can deliver more power than latter in the power stroke. 

I worked with a company testing their HHO systems on metro transit buses that ran diesel. Despite seeing encouraging real world results in diesel fuel per mile, plate deterioration and continued maintenance made it not commercially viable. 

Sadly the whole field is littered with scammers who will try to make a quick buck, and many others that dont actually understand.

replies.author: @UncleKennysPlace
replies.text: Increasing flame speed (and thus cylinder pressure, temperature, and thus power) is easy. So easy, that we re-circulate exhaust gases to keep the temps and pressures _down._ This is because high temps cause more oxides of nitrogen, and also can cause detonation.

author: @Mrhyah
text:
There's also Blue Energy/Osmotic power, which involves using salinity gradients to generate electricity. Currently it's used for mixing river and sea water, and seems infeasible for something as small as a car(the theoretical specific energy is less than 1% of a Lion battery, and actual charging efficiency is around 60%), but certainly if size is no issue you could use a salt water tank or pool as a battery/generator. There's a new version with apparently 1000 times the power output, but that's scaled off a single nano-wire device made in a lab and presumably making an actual usable size device is currently impossible.
 
I find it especially interesting because one method, where you charge and discharge a supercapacitor while varying the salt concentration of the solution, seems both doable to diy, and is in essence the same thing as capacitive and triboelectric generators.
 
It's also very similar to ionic semiconductors, which might be a potential future of computation, both in low cost of manufacturing and the fact that biological systems also run on ion exchange membranes so they could be interfaced with or even powered by biological systems.

You're ignoring the fact that refilling a tank with water is a lot faster than recharging a battery pack with electricity.

You are also ignoring the fact that using exploding water and using that energy to turn an ICE, you can use part of that energy to maintain a charged battery just as you do with petroleum ICEs.

You are also ignoring the fact that the electric grid cannot handle charging a massive fleet of electric powered automobiles over night during low demand, let alone during the day during peak demand. 

In addition, if you have a substantial fleet of electric cars charging at night, the lower cost of nighttime electricity loses it's lower cost because the demand has increased.

A water car, if possible, is therefore more advantageous than an electric car.

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: The water is only a mechanism to transfer energy, not a source of it. You can't get more energy out than you put in. Cavitation or no. You'd just have 2 things to recharge instead of one: a water tank and a battery.

replies.text: Ultrasonics! And yes the rest you mentioned is doable diy, the problem is the mess salt makes in the system. An alkaline is commonly used in electrolysis  but salt is needed to help capacitance in a very technical multichamber setup with individual balanced electrodes in each cell. The output is HHO and needs further processing for fuel cells but can be compressed and run through an aftermarket LPG auto gas mixer to run a petrol or diesel engine. The diesel needs a 16% original fuel load for ignition, and that is the reason burning hydrogen is only possible in a diesel!! A petrol engine, unless specifically designed and constructed at a high cost, will not run long on hydrogen. Carbon emitting fuels have anti-corrosive properties that are so much more vital and complex in maintaining engine and exhaust condition and functionality than people realize. Also water contamination of engine oil is a factor that need additional systems and maintenance on any engine burning hydrogen as a significant part of the fuel load.
